title: The Suicide

jokes.text: That urine sample. Giving them that, that's always a pleasure, isn't it?
jokes.setup: Jerry talking about getting a physical examination
jokes.punchline: Sarcastically calling the embarrassing urine sample 'a pleasure'
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry observational humor with perfect sarcastic delivery. Universal experience made funny through his perspective.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And they want you to give them a urine sample. So you go into the bathroom, you're in there alone, and you're supposed to fill up this little cup. It's like, how much do they need? Why do they need so much? And it's always this tiny cup too, so you're there like you're trying to land a plane in a cup.

jokes.text: I don't know what you need. I gave you whatever I had there. I got more. Whatever you need, I can get it for you. Just let me know what you need. It's no problem, I mean....
jokes.setup: Jerry describing what people say when giving urine samples
jokes.punchline: The absurd over-eagerness to provide more bodily fluids
jokes.explanation: Good observational comedy about awkward medical situations, though slightly verbose.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You go to the doctor, they say 'We need a urine sample.' So you give it to them. Then they say 'We're gonna need a little more.' A little more? What do you think I'm holdin' here? Why would I keep holdin' it? 'Oh, I got plenty more where that came from!' No one's ever excited to give more urine. 'Oh, you need more? Great! I got a whole gallon in the car!'

jokes.text: But any kind of physical test, I don't know what it is... I always click into this thing where I wanna do really well.
jokes.setup: Jerry talking about physical tests
jokes.punchline: The absurd competitive drive for medical tests
jokes.explanation: Relatable absurdity - turning medical tests into competition is uniquely Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what's crazy? Going to the doctor for a physical. They make you do all these tests, and you're competing against yourself. Like, 'Oh, my cholesterol is down!' You're happy about it. But then next year, 'Oh no, it went up!' Like you lost. Like you failed a grade in cholesterol.

jokes.text: What you heard was a cotton ball touching a piece of felt.
jokes.setup: Jerry fantasizing about excelling at hearing tests
jokes.punchline: The impossibly subtle sound he imagines hearing
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly specific and absurd. The cotton ball/felt combination is perfectly Jerry's neurotic precision.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm sitting in there, and the doctor's like, 'Can you hear this?' And I'm like, 'Yeah, I hear it.' But what I'm really thinking is, I could hear that from the parking lot. I could hear that from my apartment. I could hear that before you even turned the machine on.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So he keeps going lower and lower with the sounds, right? And I'm just nodding like, 'Oh yeah, yeah, I got that one too.' Finally he gets to this sound that's so quiet, it's like a thought. It's like someone thinking about a sound in another room.

jokes.text: We're sending the results to Washington.
jokes.setup: Continuing the superhearing fantasy
jokes.punchline: Government involvement in his hearing abilities
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation building to the presidential meeting. Concise and absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So now the government's involved. They're like, 'We need to study this. We need to understand how this works.' And I'm thinking, 'No, no, no. This is MY superhearing. You can't have it.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: But of course they get involved. They always do. Next thing you know, I'm in some lab, wearing electrodes on my head, and they're making me listen to things. 'Tell us what you hear, Mr. Seinfeld. Can you hear the ant?'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm like, 'I'm Superman, not Ant-Man. I don't hear ants!'

jokes.text: We'd like you to meet the president.
jokes.setup: The superhearing fantasy reaching its peak
jokes.punchline: Presidential recognition for hearing abilities
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the escalating hearing test fantasy. Classic Seinfeld absurdist logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: And then the President calls me into the Oval Office. He says, 'We need you. We need your superhearing to detect terrorist threats.' And I'm like, 'Mr. President, I appreciate it, but I can barely hear my own thoughts over the sound of my neighbor's shower.'

jokes.text: If you named a kid Rasputin... do you think that would have a negative effect on his life?
jokes.setup: Jerry asking about naming a child
jokes.punchline: Choosing the name of a notorious historical figure
jokes.explanation: Great random Seinfeld non-sequitur. Rasputin is a perfect choice for maximum comedic effect.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You can't name your kid after a notorious historical figure. Like, you can't name your son Adolf. But then again, if you did, that kid's gonna have to beat up every other kid in school just to clear his name.

jokes.text: No.
jokes.setup: Jerry's absurd Rasputin question
jokes.punchline: George's matter-of-fact dismissal
jokes.explanation: Perfect George response - completely missing the absurdity. One word says everything about his character.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Why would you even ask me that?

jokes.text: Well, once I didn't have dinner till, like, 9:00. I'll tell you, that was pretty rough.
jokes.setup: Elaine asking if Jerry ever fasted
jokes.punchline: Calling a late dinner 'fasting' and describing it as rough
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry - trivializing real hardship through his privileged perspective. Very character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Have you ever fasted?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I called a late dinner 'fasting' once. It was rough.

jokes.text: Give me 2 bucks. I'll do it for 2 bucks.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking Kramer to throw out garbage
jokes.punchline: Kramer immediately monetizing the simple favor
jokes.explanation: Very Kramer - turning everything into a business opportunity, even garbage duty.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Kramer, can you throw out the garbage?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That'll be fifty bucks.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? You're charging me to throw out my own garbage?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's my rate now. I'm a professional garbage consultant.

jokes.text: There's no way I'd touch that bag for less than $2.
jokes.setup: Jerry offering 50 cents
jokes.punchline: Kramer's firm pricing on garbage handling
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Kramer's bizarre business sense and standards.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'll give you 50 cents to take out the garbage.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Fifty cents? That's it? I don't think so. A dollar.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A dollar? For taking out the garbage?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's my price. Take it or leave it.

jokes.text: You're not getting no Drake's Coffee Cake for 50 cents.
jokes.setup: Jerry suggesting 50 cents could buy a Drake's Coffee Cake
jokes.punchline: Kramer's street-smart economics correction
jokes.explanation: Sets up the Drake's Coffee Cake running gag while showing Kramer's practical knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Fifty cents? You can't get a cake for fifty cents!

jokes.text: What do you need, a break from getting up at 11?
jokes.setup: Jerry questioning why George needs a vacation without a job
jokes.punchline: Pointing out George's already leisurely lifestyle
jokes.explanation: Sharp character-based insult that perfectly captures George's unemployed lifestyle. Great rhythm.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You need a vacation? From what?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I need to get away.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You don't have a job. What do you need a vacation from? You're always on vacation!

jokes.text: I can't commit to a woman, I'm not committing to an airline.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining why he won't do nonrefundable deals
jokes.punchline: Comparing airline commitment to romantic commitment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry line - commitment phobic applied to airlines. Brilliant parallel structure and very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't do nonrefundable deals. It's like when you book a flight and you realize you might not want to go. With airlines, they've got you. But with a relationship? I need to know I can get out at any time.

jokes.text: You should be ashamed of yourself. I want you out of here.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Drake's Coffee Cake pricing
jokes.punchline: Gina's dramatic overreaction to the cake discussion
jokes.explanation: Introduces Gina's dramatic personality through cake-related outrage. Sets up her character.

dialogue.speaker: Gina
dialogue.line: Wait, you're telling me Drake's Coffee Cake costs HOW much?! I can't believe this! My whole world is falling apart! Do you know what this means? This means everything I've built, everything I've worked for... it's all been a LIE!

jokes.text: Good enough.
jokes.setup: Jerry asking how Martin is doing
jokes.punchline: Martin's underwhelming response suggesting things aren't great
jokes.explanation: Economical character establishment - Martin's depression/dissatisfaction in two words.

jokes.text: He was doing standup comedy in Kennebunkport, Maine... in this really ominous nightclub. The stage was on a cliff and the audience was throwing all the comics off.
jokes.setup: George describing his dream about Martin
jokes.punchline: The absurd nightmare comedy club scenario
jokes.explanation: Surreal dream logic that's both absurd and meta-commentary on comedy. Very Seinfeldian.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I had this dream last night. I'm at the Comedy Cellar, right? And Martin Short is there performing. But he's doing my material. My jokes! And everyone's laughing hysterically. I'm sitting in the back, nobody knows who I am. Martin Short is killing with my stuff.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So I stand up, I yell 'Those are my jokes!' And Martin Short stops, looks at me, and says 'No they're not.' And the whole audience turns around and boos me. They start chanting 'Martin! Martin! Martin!'

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Then—and this is the crazy part—Martin Short walks over to me, shakes my hand, and says 'Thanks for the material, pal.' And I'm so humiliated I just leave. I walk out of the club and I'm on the street, and I realize I'm naked.

jokes.text: I think I've played there.
jokes.setup: George describing the nightmare comedy club
jokes.punchline: Jerry claiming to have performed at this impossible venue
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry response - treating the absurd as mundane professional experience. Great timing.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It's a nightmare. The stage is like three feet high, the crowd's right on top of you, heckling is relentless, the sound system is shot, and there's always some drunk guy in the front row.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, I played there.

jokes.text: Your father's bald. Baldness is inherited from the mother's side, Jerry. But your mother's bald too.
jokes.setup: George claiming he knew he'd be bald as paranormal
jokes.punchline: Jerry systematically destroying George's logic, ending with both parents being bald
jokes.explanation: Good escalating logic puzzle that deflates George's paranormal claims. Well-structured buildup.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I knew I was going to be bald. I could just feel it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: How could you feel it?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I don't know, I just had a sense about it.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Your mother's not bald.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, but my father is.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you have a fifty-fifty shot. That's not a premonition, that's a coin flip.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, I knew it was going to be me.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: How? How could you possibly know that?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I just knew.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Did your mother's father have hair?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Did your father's father have hair?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're telling me your father's father was bald, your father is bald, and somehow you're the only one who could read this in the tea leaves?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'm very intuitive.

jokes.text: I heard he used to polish off a box of Triscuits. Really? Oh, yeah. Gandhi loved Triscuits.
jokes.setup: George wondering what Gandhi ate before fasting
jokes.punchline: Jerry's completely fabricated historical 'fact' about Gandhi and crackers
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist historical revision. The specificity of 'Triscuits' makes it perfect Seinfeld humor.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I wonder what Gandhi ate before he started fasting.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Crackers.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Crackers?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, he ate a big box of crackers right before he fasted. That's historical fact.

jokes.text: It's not too bad. It's not like a Sunny von Bulow coma.
jokes.setup: Jerry commenting on Martin's coma condition
jokes.punchline: Comparing it to a famous real-world coma case as if rating comas
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that treats comas like restaurant reviews. Good cultural reference integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, Martin's been in a coma for three weeks now. That's a pretty good coma. I mean, it's no Karen Ann Quinlan, but it's solid.

jokes.text: And all indications are he does not like Drake's Coffee Cake. He said that? He was screaming about it all night. How it's too sweet and it falls apart when you eat it.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Martin's feelings toward Jerry
jokes.punchline: His hatred extending to a specific baked good with detailed complaints
jokes.explanation: Good callback that escalates the cake obsession. The detailed critique is funny character work.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Martin hates me so much, he's got opinions about my whole existence.

dialogue.speaker: Gina
dialogue.line: What kind of opinions?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: He doesn't just hate me as a person. He hates my croissants. He says they're too buttery, too flaky, they shed crumbs all over his shirt.

dialogue.speaker: Gina
dialogue.line: It's just a croissant.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Not to Martin. To Martin, it's a personal attack. He's got a whole manifesto about my baking technique.

jokes.text: I wish he was not in a coma. I wish he was dead. I would like to pull the plug out from him!
jokes.setup: Gina expressing frustration with Martin
jokes.punchline: Escalating from wishing he wasn't in a coma to wanting to kill him
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation humor. Gina's dramatic nature coming through in extreme statements.

dialogue.speaker: Gina
dialogue.line: I wish Martin wasn't in a coma. Actually, no — I wish he was dead. No wait, I wish I could kill him myself.

jokes.text: I'm a man who respects a good coma.
jokes.setup: Gina questioning Jerry's masculinity for not kissing her
jokes.punchline: Jerry's ridiculous justification treating comas with reverence
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurd reasoning that's very quotable. Perfect Jerry logic - making the ridiculous sound reasonable.

dialogue.speaker: Gina
dialogue.line: You didn't even try to kiss me.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I was going to, but then I thought about it, and I realized - you were in a coma. You can't give consent when you're in a coma. It's not right.

dialogue.speaker: Gina
dialogue.line: I came out of the coma!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, but the coma was still fresh. The coma deserves respect. You don't just jump all over someone the second they wake up from a coma. That's coma etiquette.

jokes.text: If it was one of those in-and-out comas, maybe. But when a guy's got a coma going like this... you don't wanna mess with it.
jokes.setup: Jerry's coma respect philosophy
jokes.punchline: Treating coma quality like a professional assessment
jokes.explanation: Extends the coma respect joke with technical distinctions. Very Jerry approach to absurd situations.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I have a philosophy about coma patients. I think they deserve respect. Just because you're in a coma doesn't mean you're not still you. You're still a person with dignity.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I think we should treat coma patients like we'd treat anyone else. Evaluate their performance, give them feedback, maybe a performance review.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: "Well, your unconsciousness is really coming along nicely, but we're going to need you to work on your responsiveness. The drooling is acceptable, but we need more eye movement."

jokes.text: There's no tall buildings where these people live? They can't wrap their lips around a revolver like a normal person?
jokes.setup: George discussing suicide machines
jokes.punchline: Suggesting more 'traditional' suicide methods with casual tone
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that's somewhat lazy - relies on shock value over cleverness. Typical George bluntness.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You know, suicide machines are all well and good, but sometimes you want to go the traditional route. You know, like a nice rope, or pills, or... I don't know, throwing yourself in front of a bus.

jokes.text: I give them 24 hours to get out of it. They can't get out of it by 24 hours, it's a land rush.
jokes.setup: Discussing coma etiquette and taking the victim's belongings
jokes.punchline: Kramer's arbitrary 24-hour rule and 'land rush' metaphor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Kramer logic - treating comas like mining claims. The 'land rush' metaphor is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You gotta have a rule. You can't just take things. There's gotta be a waiting period. Like, after a guy goes into a coma, you gotta wait 24 hours before you can take his stuff.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That's your rule?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's like a land rush. When the coma bell rings, you can go in and grab what you want.

jokes.text: Baldness is inherited from the mother's side, Jerry. But your mother's bald too.
jokes.setup: Rula the psychic asking about Pauline
jokes.punchline: Jerry's systematic destruction of George's paranormal baldness claim
jokes.explanation: Good callback but not as strong in this context. Jerry deflating George's psychic pretensions.

dialogue.speaker: Rula
dialogue.line: Who is Pauline?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Pauline? I don't know any Pauline.

dialogue.speaker: Rula
dialogue.line: She says you do.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, she's wrong. There's no Pauline.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Maybe it's a spirit that's been following you.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: A spirit? What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: My cousin had the same thing. Turned out to be a poltergeist that was making his hair fall out.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, you don't have any hair to fall out.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's because the poltergeist got to me first!

jokes.text: Psychics should be licensed. And it wouldn't be hard. We could just give them the regular DMV test only with the silver dollars and the pizza dough over the eyes.
jokes.setup: Jerry commenting on psychic credibility after the bad experience
jokes.punchline: Specific absurd licensing test involving pizza dough blindfolds
jokes.explanation: Creative premise with great specific details. The pizza dough/silver dollars combination is perfectly absurd.

jokes.text: Like Kreskin. Instead of just hiding his check... why don't we try stopping his check. Let's see how he handles that.
jokes.setup: Jerry continuing his psychic licensing idea
jokes.punchline: The pun on 'stopping' vs 'hiding' a check to test psychic abilities
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that builds on the psychic theme. Good cultural reference integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, I'm thinking about getting my psychic license. I could test people - I'd hide a check somewhere, and if they're a real psychic, they should be able to find it.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: A check? What if they stop you before you hide it?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Then they're not a psychic, they're just observant.

jokes.text: You can say what you want about me... but I'll be damned if I'm gonna stand here while you insult my toothbrush.
jokes.setup: Gina criticizing Jerry's toothbrush
jokes.punchline: Jerry defending his toothbrush with mock dignity
jokes.explanation: Good escalation humor - treating toothbrush criticism as a grave insult. Classic Seinfeld minutiae obsession.

dialogue.speaker: Gina
dialogue.line: That toothbrush is disgusting.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Disgusting? This toothbrush has been with me through thick and thin. It's loyal, dependable, and it gets the job done.

jokes.text: No, more like the Three Stooges at the end of every movie.
jokes.setup: Gina asking if Jerry would run like a mouse if Martin wakes up
jokes.punchline: Jerry comparing his escape to the Stooges' slapstick exits
jokes.explanation: Good visual reference that audiences can picture. Jerry's self-deprecating humor about his cowardice.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'd run like a mouse. I'd be out of there so fast, I'd leave a mouse-shaped hole in the wall like the Roadrunner. The Three Stooges would be like, 'Whoa, whoa, whoa, that's too fast!'

jokes.text: Fruit makes me incontinent.
jokes.setup: Jerry suggesting George eat fruit during his fast
jokes.punchline: George's bizarre and TMI medical excuse
jokes.explanation: Perfect George line - oversharing medical information as casual excuse. Very economical and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I can't eat fruit. I'm lactose intolerant.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Fruit doesn't have lactose.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I know, but I can't digest it. It comes right through me. You should see the toilet.

jokes.text: The carpet sweeper is the biggest scam perpetrated on the American public since One Hour Martinizing.
jokes.setup: Elaine defending carpet sweepers against Jerry's dismissal
jokes.punchline: Comparing carpet sweepers to dry cleaning as major American scams
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor with specific cultural reference. The Martinizing comparison is dated but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Carpet sweepers are not a scam! They work great.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: They don't work. You push them around and nothing happens.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's because you don't know how to use them. You have to have the right technique.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Technique? It's a carpet sweeper, not a sport.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Well, carpet sweepers are right up there with dry cleaning. Two of the biggest scams in America.

jokes.text: That's your big boy.
jokes.setup: Newman commenting on the full-size Drake's Coffee Cake
jokes.punchline: Newman's reverent description of the cake size
jokes.explanation: Good Newman food obsession moment. The 'big boy' phrasing is perfectly character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Look at the size of this thing! It's not a cake, it's a CAKE. This isn't a snack, this is a commitment. You don't eat this cake, you schedule time off work to eat this cake. This cake comes with its own zip code.

jokes.text: I don't like to give out bites.
jokes.setup: Newman being asked for a bite of his cake
jokes.punchline: Newman's food hoarding policy stated as formal principle
jokes.explanation: Perfect Newman food selfishness expressed as reasonable policy. Very character-specific humor.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: No! This is my cake. I don't share my food with anyone. That's my policy.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: You see, I have a very strict food hoarding protocol. Everything I have is mine, and nothing is yours.

jokes.text: You know, they're so fragile.
jokes.setup: Newman explaining why he won't share his cake
jokes.punchline: Treating the cake like a delicate artifact
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Martin's cake complaints, now used as Newman's excuse. Character-appropriate food obsession.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: This cake is a Laurens-Strauss original. It's a one-of-a-kind creation. I'm not gonna just slice it up and pass it around like it's some Sara Lee.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's a cake, Newman. It's supposed to be eaten.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Not this cake. This cake is art. You don't eat art.

jokes.text: You wouldn't believe it. Rasputin.
jokes.setup: Jerry being asked what Rula named her baby
jokes.punchline: The return of the Rasputin name from the opening
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback that brings the episode full circle. The absurd name actually happening adds great payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: She named him Rasputin.

jokes.text: The thing I don't understand about the suicide person... is the people that try and commit suicide for some reason, they don't die, and then that's it. They stop trying.
jokes.setup: Jerry's closing standup about suicide attempts
jokes.punchline: Questioning why people give up on suicide after one failed attempt
jokes.explanation: Dark observational humor that's somewhat obvious. Sets up the bigger punchline that follows.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Why do people give up on suicide after one failed attempt? If at first you don't succeed, shouldn't you keep trying?

jokes.text: Because they give up too easy. I say pills don't work, try a rope. Car won't start in the garage? Get a tune-up. There's nothing more rewarding than reaching a goal you've set for yourself.
jokes.setup: Jerry explaining why suicide attempters should persist
jokes.punchline: Treating suicide like any other goal with practical advice and motivational ending
jokes.explanation: Dark humor with good escalation and ironic motivational twist. The car metaphor works well for the closing bit.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, if you're going to do it, you gotta commit. You can't just half-ass it. That's like anything else in life. You want to lose weight? You gotta stick with it. You want to learn guitar? Practice every day. You want to kill yourself? Same thing—you gotta follow through. Don't give up after the first attempt. Maybe you weren't holding the rope right. Maybe you need a better chair. Learn from your mistakes. Adjust your technique. And hey, if at first you don't succeed... well, you know the rest. You got this!

tmdb_overview: A psychic warns George to cancel his vacation; Jerry is hit on by his neighbor's girl after the neighbor's suicide attempt; Elaine fasts.
insight: Dark premise undermines laugh count; escalation-heavy structure delivers 40 jokes across 23 minutes.
